A-2. Delayed reporting and recognition of completed PPE totaling P8.141 million in the books of accounts.
The International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 17 provides that the cost of an item of property, plant, and equipment shall be recognized as an asset if, and only if: (a) it is probable that future economic benefits or service potential associated with the item will flow to the entity; and (b) the cost or fair value of the item can be measured reliably.
We obtained information on the completed infrastructure projects for the calendar year (CY) 2024 and compared the same with those reported in GLs of the LGU. Based on the comparison, we determined that completed projects per the Municipal Engineer’s records totaled P8.901 million (Appendix D ) and were composed of five projects, while those reported by the Municipal Accountant amounted to P17.042 million (Appendix E) and were composed of 10 projects, resulting in a difference of P8.141 million. The specific details of the difference are identified below:
We inspected the above-completed projects and confirmed with the Municipal Engineer that they  were issued Certificates of Completion; thus there is no doubt that these projects are indeed completed as of year-end.
We also inquired with the Municipal Accountant to clarify the difference in amounts recorded for the year, and we noted that completed projects were recorded when paid and, in the case of year-end transactions, when obligated. Thus, due to timing differences, projects were reported at different periods depending on the offices providing the information.
While there is no doubt that the PPE recorded in the books are completed projects, there remains differences in valuation due to the timing differences observed. The accuracy of reported depreciation expense and the related accumulated depreciation affect the net book value reported, thus resulting in misstatements in PPE.
Recommendations
We recommended, and the Management agreed, that the Municipal Accountant:
In coordination with the Municipal Engineer, perform reconciliation procedures at year-end to determine all completed projects to be recognized in the books regardless of the status of billing of the contractor; and
Determine the effect of timing differences on depreciation expenses and accumulated depreciation, and prepare the corresponding adjusting journal entries.
Continuous recognition of unidentified items in the PPE accounts totaling P7.838 million in lump sum, without any disclosure on the nature of these accounts.
Examination of the detailed PPE schedule includes lump-sum items carried over from CY 2002 totaling P7.838 million; details are as follows:
The Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) disclosed that the above items were continuously recorded in the books as there were no available documents to support any further action in relation to these balances. The amounts were from the previous accountants, who cannot provide details as to descriptions, acquisition costs, and serviceable lives of the properties. Thus, the continuous recognition of these assets as PPE, without any disclosure as to their nature, misstates the PPE balances. Further, since information on the assets is also incomplete, depreciation expenses also cannot be computed.
Since these were in the books for more than 23 years and are unidentifiable, it is highly probable that these PPE items are fully depreciated or even unserviceable; therefore, they should be derecognized in the FS provided that the procedures on derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs is complied, Otherwise, accountability issues over these PPE may be raised.

Municipal Accountant draw a journal entry voucher (JEV) to adjust/correct the affected accounts if necessary.
Inclusion of tangible items costing below the capitalization threshold totaling P5.755 million contrary to COA Circular No. 2022-004 and affecting the inventory and government equity accounts.
Section 23 of RA No. 11639, also known as the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for Financial Year 2022, states that “Tangible items below Fifty Thousand Pesos (P50,000) shall be accounted as semi-expendable property”. With this, COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, was issued to provide the guidelines on its implementation relative to the increase in the capitalization threshold from P15,000.00 to P50,000.00.
Moreover, semi-expendable property refers to tangible items that meet the definition and recognition criteria of PPE but are below the capitalization threshold of P50,000.00. This shall be considered as a change in accounting policy that requires retrospective application, such that:
For issued tangible items acquired prior to 2024 previously classified as PPE, the carrying amount shall be expensed/charged to the prior period adjustment and Government Equity accounts. The corresponding accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment loss shall be closed in the books of accounts;
Tangible items previously classified as PPE, which are still in the custody of the General Services Office (GSO) shall be reclassified to the appropriate semi-expendable property account; and
For CY 2024 onwards, semi-expendable property shall be recorded as inventory upon acquisition and as expense upon issuance to end-user.
Review of the PPE schedule as of December 31, 2024 disclosed that various individual items in the PPE have recognition costs below P50,000.00, as follows (details are in Appendix F):
Analysis of the LGU’s FS, Notes to FS (NFS) and JEVs prepared by the Accounting Department show that all purchases with costs below P50,000.00 were recognized as part of the PPE account for CY 2024 and earlier. With the increase in the capitalization threshold, various tangible items in CY 2024 with a total acquisition cost of P1.178 million were still recorded as PPE.
Further, for CY 2023 and earlier purchases, no adjustment was made on the affected accounts particularly on the reclassification of tangible items from PPE to the Inventories account and expending the carrying amounts of issued semi-expendable items. The non-adjustment was due to the absence of data from the designated General Services Officer (GSO) pertaining to semi-expendable properties in her custody and those issued to the respective end-users supported with Property Acknowledgment Receipts (PARs) and the non-disposal of unserviceable PPEs. Verification showed that most of the items were fully depreciated and status/existence of these could not be determined due to incomplete inventory taking.
As a result of improper recording of the above-mentioned items, the PPE accounts are overstated due to: a) the non-reclassification to applicable expense accounts of currently issued PPE and, b) non-dropping from PPE the net carrying amount of previously issued items costing more than P15,000.00 but below P50,000.00.
The non-categorization of semi-expendable properties would have helped in the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances as encouraged in COA Circular 2020-006. Had the increase in capitalization threshold been implemented, the Agency’s related records would have been updated and would have had a corresponding decrease in PPE. It would also segregate and monitor the accountability of end-users/custodians over the said properties accordingly and would have also helped determine the appropriate means of extinguishing the said accountability, as provided under Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 2022-004.
Recommendations
We recommended, and the Management agreed that the:
Accounting Office apply the criteria on capitalization of PPE and make the necessary adjustments to correct and/or restate the balances of affected accounts and ensure proper classification of assets in the books; and
GSO Designate or the Municipal Treasurer:
Provide data to the Accounting Office relevant to items reclassified as semi-expendable property including information of its condition;
Update existing PPE and Inventory records and prepare and maintain the additional necessary forms/reports/registries for purposes of record-keeping and installation of controls over semi-expendable properties provided in Section 4 of the Circular; and
Use the Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for issuance of semi-expandable items to establish monitoring, control, and accountability over them.
Challenges in the conduct of regular physical count of LGU’s properties
The COA Circular No. 80-124 dated January 18, 1980 states that, “Physical inventory-taking being an indispensable procedure for checking the integrity of property custodianship has to be regularly enforced. In line with this and to operationalize the provisions of Section 102 of the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines [P.D. 1445], this circular is issued.”
The same circular also states that, “All inventory reports shall be prepared on the prescribed form (Gen. Form No. 41-A) and certified correct by the committee in charge thereof, noted by the Auditor and approved by the head of the agency. The reports shall be properly reconciled with accounting and inventory records.”
Our review of the LGU’s consolidated FS as of December 31, 2024 showed the following PPE account balances:
The data above showed that the total value of the PPE as at year-end is P452.671 million, however, the audit team has yet to receive a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to be prepared, verified, and submitted by the LGU’s Inventory Committee.
In an interview conducted with the Municipal Treasurer, the head of inventory committee, it was revealed that the Municipal Accountant was unable to furnish the complete property records thereby, hindering the said committee from continuing the inventory count of LGU’s PPE. Even if the said records were given, the RPCPPE cannot be finalized due to unfinished inventory taking. They encountered various challenges, including but not limited to incomplete records, identification issues as PPE items were not labelled/tagged, difficult-to-access locations of several PPE items, loss/non-existing PPE items, actual physical condition, complexity in the coordination with various agencies and departments, possible interruption of operation and limited availability of manpower, time and other resources.
Physical inventory taking is essential in determining the accuracy and correctness of the PPE account being recorded in the financial statements. Its purpose it to validate the existence, whereabouts, and conditions of the property listed in the accounting records. As a result of the unfinished inventory taking and non-preparation of the RPCPPE, it casts doubt on the reliability and correctness of the PPE account.
PPE comprises 82 per cent of the total Assets of the LGU, thus, the amount presented in the financial statements are vital in decision making and are to be presented for transparency. Internal controls for PPE are vital for maintaining accurate financial reporting, safeguarding assets, and ensuring compliance with regulations. Controls shall be placed to ensure that PPE items are accurately recorded in financial statements, reflecting their true value and condition. In asset management like regular physical counts, tagging, and monitoring, LGU can safeguard PPE items from theft, loss, or unauthorized use.
Recommendations
We recommended, and the LCE agreed, to ensure that:
LGU’s Inventory Team to conduct planning addressing all identified challenges to fast track and complete the PPE inventory, ensure the execution of the planned inventory taking strategy and to facilitate the conduct of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts by performing the following:
Identify other departments within the LGU that may have available staff with relevant skills (e.g., record-keeping, accounting, or logistics) who can be temporarily reassigned to assist and focus with the inventory process.
Collaborate with other government agencies that can provide training on inventory management, especially on the full implementation of the 'One-time Cleansing of PPE Accounts,' as outlined in COA Circular 2020-006, so that the LGU may be fully aware of the primary guidelines of the said circular.
Establish a new set of Inventory Committee, with members temporarily relieved of their regular duties until the completion of the physical inventory within a clear and agreed-upon timeline.
Ensure that the newly reconstituted Inventory Committee prepare and submit the approved Physical Inventory Plan to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of the inventory-taking process.
LGU’s Inventory Team’s inventory report be reconciled with the accounting and property records to verify the existence of the LGU’s properties and submit the required report to the Audit Team. Any discrepancies found should be investigated;
Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to adjust/correct the affected accounts if necessary; and
All employees and departments to formulate and implement stringent internal controls for PPE for ensuring the integrity and to maintain accurate financial reporting, safeguarding assets from theft, loss, or unauthorized use and ensuring compliance with regulations.

Recommendations
We recommended, and the LCE agreed, to monitor and ensure that the :
Municipal Development Council and other concerned personnel strengthen and enhance project planning in the entire LGU, particularly in setting attainable timelines for project implementation to plan and identify the priority projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan. For loan amortization payments, obtain and maintain loan amortization schedules to identify sufficient appropriation; and
Municipal Engineer monitor and implement all projects during the year to ensure the optimum utilization of the 20 per cent DF.

Recommendations
We recommended, and the LCE agreed, to monitor and ensure that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS):
Fully utilize GAD budget allocation by providing adequate financial assistance, facilities and services, to address gender issues and the GAD mandate of the LGU;
Plan thoroughly the schedule of GAD activities to ensure that these are not in conflict with the schedule of other activities;
Obtain the commitments of the project implementers in ensuring the attainment of the goals through proper planning and establish an effective monitoring and evaluation system to monitor the implementation of the annual GPB; and
Prepare and establish the GAD budget by attribution, integrating the GAD perspective in major programs as a means of gradually increasing the allocation for the GAD Budget and the gender responsiveness of the agency.
Management Comment
The GAD focal person admitted that she does not have the necessary expertise in integrating GAD into other priority PPAs of the LGU.

Recommendations
We recommended, and the LCE agreed, to provide adequate and available resources for the collection and processing of data requirements of the GAD Database.
We also recommended, and the GFPS agreed to:
Create and institutionalize the GAD Database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data to provide the basis for gender analysis, planning, programming, and policy formulation to effectively plan and implement GAD-related PAPs;
Coordinate with the various agencies, departments, and barangays in the updating of the GAD database to serve as a basis for gender-responsive planning, programming, and policy formulation;
Establish a linkage between the existing Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS) Accelerated Poverty Profiling and the GAD sex-disaggregated data or GAD database required under PCW MC No. 2014-05 dated 24 November 2014, to catalyze performance-based gender-responsive planning on the basis of a reliable information system; and
Ensure that the gender issues contained in the ensuing GPB are based on the results of gender analysis conducted on the GAD Database and/or any other gender analysis tools.

Remaining contract value of infrastructure projects without obligation and make necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of unobligated allotment as of December 31, 2024;
Contracts/POs in full amount after perfection of the contracts/POs for all infrastructure projects and goods, and not every time payments are processed; and
Multi-year contracts in proportion of the approved appropriation of the current year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of each year a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the preparation of the necessary accounting entries to set-up the actual Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant revisit Sections 36 and 93 of the NGAS Manual Volume 1 on the pro–forma accounting entries for income, collection and deposit, including pertinent entries for Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax.

We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to prioritize and complete the physical inventory within a reasonable period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. Also, management is enjoined to ensure the full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in the same Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Assessor review the Assessment Roll to correct the noted discrepancies concerning properties with duplicate/identical Property Identification Number, properties with zero market value and properties with zero assessed value.

We further recommended that an updated data on real properties within the municipality be submitted to the Municipal Treasurer for the preparation/updating of the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card, copy furnished the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management submit full liquidation and refund all unutilized balances to the source agency for long outstanding but completed programs, projects and activities.

We also recommended that the balances/savings of LGU Counterparts for completed projects be transferred to the General Fund to fund for new projects of the municipality.

Further, we recommended that Management fast track the implementation of the intended PPAs from previous years’ fund transfers.

We recommended that Management expedite the hiring of personnel to fill-up these vacant positions and/or fast track the promotion of deserving/qualified workers among the present pool of manpower resources in the municipality.

We recommended that information on the loss and damage to properties be furnished to the Municipal Accountant to disclose the details in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We further recommended that Management require all department/offices to submit a list of lost and damaged properties to the General Service Office. The lists must be traced to the Property Acknowledgment Receipt/ Acknowledgment Receipt of Equipment for the preparation of the requests from relief of accountability (for lost items) or Inventory and Inspection of Unserviceable Property (for damaged items due for disposal).

We recommended that Management limit the hiring of casuals/job order employees for intermittent, emergency jobs or specific piece of work with short duration as guided by CSC-COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; and maximize the services of the permanent employees for regular agency functions.

We also recommended that Management direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to charge and record the wages of the contractual and job order workers performing regular agency functions to Personal Services.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant follow the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting for supplies to establish control in the receipt and disposition of the inventory items.

We also recommended that the Property Officer prepare and submit Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) to the accounting office as basis in preparing a journal entry to record the issuances/expenses.

We further recommended that a physical count of all undistributed/unconsumed items be conducted to determine the cost of the items that will be restored back to the inventory account.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ascertain whether or not these payable accounts under the Trust Fund are still due for remittance to concerned offices.  If these are still due for remittance, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to remit immediately the amount involved in order to protect the interest of the employee-members concerned and clear the books of these prior years’ payable accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strengthen cash transactions' efficiency and effectiveness by implementing effective internal control.

Furthermore, the Municipal Treasure must observe 

1. The cashbook balance must be reconciled with the cash on hand at the close of each day.

2. Cashbook balances must be reconciled with the subsidiary ledgers at least once every quarter.

We recommended that all the collectors faithfully adhere to the existing rules and regulations on the recording of the receipt and remittance of all collections handled by them to facilitate monitoring and verification thereof, prevent loss and/or misuse of government funds and avoid possible shortage in audit, which has penal sanctions. 

In addition, we recommend that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that all of the deficiencies noted above will be rectified or corrected.

We recommended that Management:

a. Construct a warehouse where all movable procured properties will be delivered and stored prior its issuance to the end users.

b. Appoint/designate a full time General Service Officer to appropriately manage the Property, Plant, and Equipment and other properties of the municipality.

c. Reconstitute an Inventory Committee and to observe the procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 pertaining to missing/non-existing property. The disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs should be strictly implemented in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, so that they be properly disposed of and dropped from the books of accounts of the agency.

We recommended that the Accounting Office:

1. Apply the criteria on capitalization of PPE as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2022-004 to ensure proper classification of assets in the books;  

2. Make the necessary adjustments to correct and/or restate the balances of affected accounts.

We recommend that the GSO Designate:

1. Provide data to the Accounting Office relevant to items reclassified as semi-expendable property including information on their condition.

2. Update existing PPE and Inventory records and prepare and maintain the additional necessary forms/reports/registries for purposes of record-keeping and installation of controls over semi-expendable properties provided in Section 4 of the Circular; and

3. Onwards, use the ICS for issuance of semi-expandable items to establish monitoring, control, and accountability over them.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer be directed to prepare the Report on the Local Road Network and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System with the Inventory Committee's help. 

Furthermore, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintain the subsidiary ledger for roads and their components, reconcile it with the result of the physical count at year-end, correctly depreciate the accounts, and disclose it in the Notes to the Financial Statements as prescribed.

We recommended that the LCE requires:

a. The Municipal Treasurer to conduct an inventory count of these various Covid-19 vaccines, drugs, medicines, and other medical supplies received to determine what is left of these inventories;

b. the MHO to inform the accounting of any receipt of medical supplies and inventory from any sources and forward the documents as proof of receipt by the municipality to be the basis for recording in the book of accounts; and

c. the Municipal Accountant to record the Inventories of medical supplies and medicines still available at the end of the year 2022 in the book of accounts of the LGU.

16.9	We recommended that the management:

a. Establish hiring policies/guidelines and criteria (designation, function, qualification, specific/ lump sum/intermittent work/job and services, etc.); and

b. Evaluate the staffing pattern of the municipality to fill up the vacant and funded positions of the LGU in order to limit the hiring of contract orders and job order employees and be guided by the pertinent provisions of the Civil Service Commission.

We recommended and management agreed that:

Moving forward, Management should be observant and strictly abide the prescribed laws, rules, and regulations in revising the Annual Budget and Annual Investment Programs.

A. For the Accounting Department/ Municipal Accountant:

To record expenses based on the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) together with the supporting RIS.

B. Municipal Treasurer as the General Services Officer:

To consolidate the RIS in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to correctly record its issuances to reflect the correct Inventory and Expense balances.
We also recommend that the Municipality of Alicia provide a suitable warehouse and a responsible person to monitor the receipts and issuances of materials procured.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant recompute and that Management demand liquidated damages for the 78 days of delay in the delivery of the manlift truck.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the suppliers to include the brand names in their quotations.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to indicate the brand names and complete specification of the goods procured in the Purchase Order and Contract Agreements.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the following documents to establish the validity and propriety of the disbursements made in connection with the training allowances for basketball players and the coaching staff:
a. Clear-cut guidelines in the selection of players;
b. Contract between the Municipality of Alicia and the coaching staff and players, providing for conditions that would benefit both the players and the local government; and

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant revisit the provisions of the IPSAS and other COA issuances, particularly on the preparation and presentation of accounts in the FS.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
Undertake revenue-enhancing strategies and cost-cutting measures to generate/increase revenues to reduce and/or eliminate the cash deficit.
Require that the Local Finance Committee and other officials concerned regularly monitor the Municipality’s cash position; and,
Ensure that trust liabilities are used solely for their intended purpose.

Moving forward, we recommended that the Management agreed to use 5 per cent VAT in the derivation of the agency’s cost estimates as per DPWH DO 197 series of 2016.

We revised our prior year recommendation and Management agreed that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Office of the Auditor for easy and prompt audit verification.
Furthermore, that management direct the HRMO to hire personnel to complete the plantilla of personnel for the Municipal Treasurer's Office so that the office can perform all its current duties and responsibilities.

Otherwise, direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately withhold the payment of their salaries or any amount due them.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the variance of the Loans Payable and request the assistance of a DBP representative, if needed, to substantiate and prepare the necessary adjusting entries.

We further recommended that Management make periodic reconciliation with the creditor-bank to ensure that the Loans Payable account is free from material errors and can be relied upon by the end-users of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the following adjusting journal entries, pending submission of full liquidation:

General Fund:

We further recommended that Management return the unutilized balance of the fund transfer and submit the corresponding liquidation reports of the completed LGSF-AM project.

We recommended that Management provide justification on the deficiency discussed on the non-submission of COVID-19 Vaccination Reports, and submit the remaining reports together with the corresponding Master List.

We further recommended that Management instruct all departments and offices concerned to discontinue the practice of setting a scope limitation to the audit conducted by the COA Audit Team, either by the non-submission of reports or the unnecessary delay on its submission.

We recommended that Management prioritize the implementation of the projects funded by the 20% Development Fund, and ensure that identified projects are implemented within the year to deliver the intended benefits and to get the maximum value of the fund considering the increasing prices of materials and labor costs.

We recommended that Management submit justification and supporting documents on the deficiencies discussed.

We also recommended that Management prepare and maintain sufficient and updated data of all its constituents to facilitate the immediate identification of affected families during calamities as part of its mechanism for the immediate delivery of assistance.

We further recommended that subsequent transfers be covered by a memorandum of agreement as required under the Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions.

We recommended that Management discontinue the delegation of unrelated duties and responsibilities to the Internal Auditor and prioritize the discharge of the intended function of the Internal Audit Service.

We also recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), per discussion with the concerned employee, formulate an Individual Development Plan (IDP) to document the needed steps that will be undertaken to improve or enhance performance of individual employees to address identified performance gaps and provide opportunities for enhancing competencies.

Moreover, we recommended that the Internal Auditor attend seminars on government expenditures and Republic Act No. 9184, and join capacity-building trainings on Internal Auditing Standards for the Philippine Public Sector (IASPPS) and on Internal Audit Manual (IAM) for LGUs to fully discharge its mandated functions.

We recommended that the practice of transferring the barangay shares of RPT and CTC from General Fund to Trust Fund be discontinued immediately, and that subsequent actual release of funds to the barangays be made directly in the General Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain separate Subsidiary Ledgers for each barangay under the Due to LGUs – RPT Share and Due to LGUs – CTC Share in the General Fund.

Further, we recommended that the barangay shares of RPT and CTC be remitted/released on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter pursuant to Section 271(d) of Republic Act 7160 to enable the concerned barangays to use these funds to implement their planned programs and projects.

We recommended that the LDRRM Officer promptly submit the monthly Report on Utilization of DRRMF using the required format, duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management require all concerned officials and employees to immediately refund the amounts due from them for the deficient withholding taxes.
We further recommended that Management discontinue immediately the practice of accommodating requests from officials and employees for the non-deduction of the withholding taxes on compensation and other benefits in compliance with Section 79(A) of the National Internal Revenue Code of 1997.

We recommended that Management assign personnel who will prepare the report and submit as soon as possible its AAPSI for the AAR 2020.

Further, we recommended that Management comply with the provision and submit the action plan within sixty (60) days upon receipt of the AAR in the ensuing years.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive issue an executive order reconstituting the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), and enumerating the general functions as well as their roles and responsibilities under Section 4.1.A of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We also recommended that all members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads undertake capacity building on GAD, which shall include but not limited to gender sensitivity training, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis and GAD tools.

We further recommended that Management, through the GFPS, initiate the development of GAD Database and three-year GAD Agenda to achieve a gender-responsive local governance.

We recommended that Management make written representation to the Bureau of Internal Revenue to (a) ascertain whether the activities are taxable or not; (b) apply for an Authority to Print (ATP) for receipts or invoices and pay the Annual Registration Fee, if necessary; (c) determine the taxes due, if applicable.

We recommended that Management submit justification for the non-creation of all the mandatory positions of the Local DRRM Office.

Further, we recommended that the Management initiate the creation of the two required positions and request the Sangguniang Bayan to pass a Resolution creating the same in accordance to Section 12 of RA 10121.

We recommended that Management re-organize the Local Council for the Protection of Children to advocate child rights, plans and recommends interventions and monitors children’s programs and projects in the locality.

We also recommended that one percent (1%) of the Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) be allocated to ensure that all plans for children are provided with funds, pursuant to Section 15 of Republic Act 9344.

We recommended that Management and the Vice Mayors’ League of the Philippines (VMLP) – Bohol Chapter submit legal basis on the financial aid on top of the term-membership fee and to submit justification on the relevance of the activities in the Activity Design in the discharge of its primary function concerning local legislation, otherwise, the financial aid will be disallowed/discontinued.

We recommended that Management require the VMLP and PCL to submit the basis in using multiple sets of receipts and the issuance of new sets of receipts contrary to the agreement made during the December 17, 2014 COA and ULAP meeting.

Further, advise the Municipal Treasurer to not accept the old receipts of the leagues and allow only the use of official receipts procured from the National Printing Office before the release of the membership fees, annual dues and registration fees.

We recommended the following:
 
1. Strictly enforce the liquidation of all cash advances within thirty (30) days upon return to the permanent official station;

2. A demand letter shall be made by the Municipal Accountant for the immediate liquidation and settlement of all cash advances outstanding as of December 31, 2022, from concerned officials and employees, including the suspension of salaries; and

3. Strictly adhere to applicable rules and regulations in the grant, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) directs the concerned Officers and/or employees to deposit the amount with the Authorized Government Depository bank immediately.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive directs the responsible office to comply with the general guidelines, reporting and monitoring the information and publicity on Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) as required in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 31, 2013. In line with this, the management shall:

(a) Submit to the Audit Team the list of all ongoing government Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year, 

(b) Inform this Office within ten (10) days after the infrastructure project's award or before starting the program/ activity that the appropriate project signboards and public notices are already posted,

(c) and submit to this Office the monthly monitoring report on PPAs.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant be directed to (a) prepare the necessary adjusting JEVs to reclassify the accounts, and 

(b) establish a mechanism to ensure the transactions are recorded in an appropriate account classification.

We recommended that management:

a) Temporarily halt the RCC from receiving collections from the constituents, and

b) Immediately process the RCCs Fidelity Bond as required by RA 7160.

We recommended that the LCE directs the Municipal Accountant to:

a) Strictly observe the accounting, reporting, and full disclosure requirements of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) according to COA Circular 2012-002; and, 

b) Regularly prepare and update a subsidiary ledger for this account to support the balances that appeared in the financial statements;

c) Make available to the audit team the Subsidiary Ledger to support the balances of the Trust- Liabilities-DRRMF account in accordance with the above rules and regulations.

We recommended and the management agreed that the accountable officer faithfully adheres to the existing rules and regulations on the recording and reporting of the receipts and payments of all cash advances handled by her to facilitate monitoring and verification thereof, prevent loss and/or misuse of government funds and avoid possible shortage in audit, which has penal sanctions.

We recommended and management agreed to direct the BAC Secretariat to enroll in the GPPB Online Portal and post the necessary information thereat and in conspicuous places of the LGU in support of transparency and accountability in the conduct of its procurement for ancillary supplies.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive create a PPE Physical Inventory committee with the composition stated above to (a) conduct yearly actual physical inventory or verification of all PPE of the Municipality; and (b) prepare and submit the result of the factual verification through RPCPPE to the audit team.

We recommend the following:
 
Strictly enforce the liquidation of all cash advances within thirty (30) days upon return to the permanent official station;
 
Demand letter shall be made by the Municipal Accountant for the immediate liquidation and settlement of all cash advances outstanding as of December 31, 2020, from concerned officials and employees, including the suspension of salaries; and
 
Strictly adhere to applicable rules and regulations in the grant, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the concerned Officers and/or employees to deposit amount with the Authorized Government Depository bank immediately.



Moving forward, we recommend that the LCE require the accountable officer to strictly observe the rules and regulations pertaining to the collections and deposits of the government funds.

We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer strengthen cash transactions' efficiency and effectiveness by implementing effective internal control. Furthermore, the Municipal Treasurer must observe the following:

There must be a clear-cut separation of the cashiering function from the accounting function.
The Municipal Treasurer/Liquidating Officer must record the collections and deposits daily in the cash book.
Collections must be deposited intact and as frequently as required by regulations.

The cashbook balance must be reconciled with the cash on hand at the close of each day.
Cashbook balances must be reconciled with the subsidiary ledgers at least once every quarter.
Monthly reports of accountability for accountable forms must be prepared and submitted.
	
The Accountable Form Custodian must adequately control the ordering, storing, issuing, and recording of unused official receipts and other accountable forms.
The person who made a deposit must be escorted by armed guards and provided with transportation equipment.

A responsible officer of the agency must closely supervise the collecting officers.

The stock of unused checks must be accessible only to custodians and those who prepare and sign checks. Voided or spoiled checks must be marked cancelled and held available for subsequent inspection.
Cash advances of the Disbursing Officer must not exceed the actual amount to be disbursed.

We recommend the following to the management:

A. For the Accounting Department/ Municipal Accountant:
To record the remaining items of the relief goods as inventory as stated below:

	Welfare Goods for Distribution		xxx
       		   Welfare Goods Expense			xxx

To record purchases under the Inventory account based on the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and other supporting documents such as Purchase Orders (POs), Delivery Receipts, or Supplier's Invoice.

To record expenses based on the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) together with the supporting RIS.

B. Municipal Treasurer as the General Services Officer:

To consolidate the RIS in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Office of the Municipal Accountant to correctly record its issuances to reflect the correct Inventory and Expense balances.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary correcting entries to correct the unfavorable effects of the errors made. The suggested entries are the following:

To record procurement of construction materials to be used in building the project

To record Construction Materials which are not yet issued for construction but already recorded as construction in progress

We also recommend that the Municipality of Alicia provide a suitable warehouse and a responsible person to monitor the receipts and issuances of materials procured.

We recommend that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records in order to come up with more reliable costing of the PPE accounts; and moreover, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant be directed to draw journal entry voucher to adjust/correct the affected accounts if necessary.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant provide a detailed information regarding the CIP accounts to have a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements and to prepare and maintain CIP Ledger Cards (CIPLCs) on a per project basis and to effect the transfer of completed projects, if any, from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts and ensure that proper accounts are used in recording transactions.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant be directed to (a) prepare the necessary adjusting JEVs to reclassify the accounts; and (b) to establish mechanism to ensure the transactions is recorded in an appropriate account classification.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant be directed to regularly reconcile the balances of reciprocal accounts in the financial statements. Further, we recommend that the Accountant make a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled amounts and make the necessary corrections in the books of accounts to present the corrected balances of the affected accounts.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintain a separate special account in the General Fund for loans wherein the receipts and expenditures thereof are recorded so that accurate and separate information on the status of loans were readily available for decision making purposes and observed timely payments of its loan amortization and interests due to avoid incurrence of penalties on the failure of such.

It is also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant be directed to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the details of the Loans Payable – Domestic account to provide clarity and divulge information that are not readily available on the face of the financial statements as prescribed.

We recommended that the LCE directs the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the rules and regulations on the preparation of the financial statements. Also, the Municipal Accountant to revisit the provisions of the IPSAS and other COA issuances particularly on the preparation and presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommend that the BAC or BAC Secretariat submit the contracts together with the documentary requirements enumerated in the checklists for submission to the COA Audit Team for the auditorial and legal review and ultimately to the COA Technical Audit Services depending on the amount involved.

We recommend that the General Services Officer (GSO) prepare the Report on Local Road Network and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System with the Inventory Committee's help. Furthermore, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintain the subsidiary ledger for roads and their components, reconcile it with the result of the physical count at year-end, correctly depreciate the accounts, and disclose it in the Notes to the Financial Statements as prescribed.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant review the entries made and prepare the necessary correcting entries to correct the accounts affected to come up with a fairly presented financial statements

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make correcting entry recognizing an asset account "Due from Local Government Units" for the funds transferred to the barangays of the Municipality. Also, to follow up the liquidation report of the BLGUs and ensure that the funds are utilized only for the purpose it was intended.
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer submit all the required monthly financial reports necessary in the audit on or before the due date.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive be compliant with the general guidelines, reporting and monitoring the information and publicity on Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) as imposed in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 31, 2013. In line with this, the management shall (a) Submit to the Audit Team the list of all on-going government Programs/Projects/Activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year, (b) Inform this Office within ten (10) days after the infrastructure project's award or before starting the program/ activity that the appropriate project signboards and public notices are already posted, (c) and submit to this Office the monthly monitoring report on PPAs.

Recommendation
We recommend that management create an Inventory Committee, who are temporarily relieved from all their regular duties, to complete the physical inventory in three (3) months or less, and to prepare and submit the approved Physical Inventory Plan to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of the inventory-taking activities; and ensure full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in COA Circular 2020-006. Further, direct the pertinent official to comply with the guidelines set in Section 5.1 of COA Circular No. 2018-002 to insure the properties with the GIF.

Recommendation
We recommended and management agreed that the LCE directs the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records in order to come up with more reliable costing of the PPE accounts; and moreover, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant be directed to draw a journal entry voucher to adjust/correct the affected accounts if necessary.

Recommendations
We recommended and the management agreed that the accountable officer faithfully adheres to the existing rules and regulations on the recording and reporting of the receipts and payments of all cash advances handled by her to facilitate monitoring and verification thereof, prevent loss and/or misuse of government funds and avoid possible shortage in audit, which has penal sanctions.

Recommendation
We recommended and the management agreed that all the collectors were required to faithfully adhere to the existing rules and regulations on recording the receipt and remittance of all collections handled by them to facilitate monitoring and verification thereof, prevent loss and/or misuse of government funds, and avoid possible shortage in audit, which has penal sanctions

Recommendations
We recommended and the management agreed to instruct the Municipal Engineer to require the contractor to post signage / billboard at the project site as soon as the award has been made and has to see to it that it is in accordance with the standard form as required under COA Circular 2013-004. Moreover, the Local Chief Executive shall inform the concerned Supervising Auditor and Audit Team Leader on the posting within ten (10) days after award of the project as stipulated in Section 3.1 of the same circular.

Recommendation
We recommended and the management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer/ Supply Officer to ensure that all serviceable transportation equipment owned by the Municipality are permanently marked “For Official Use Only” (3 inches) under the name/logo of the Local Government of Alicia.

Recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that:
The LDC prepare and submit approved updated AIP; and
Moving forward, Management should be observant and strictly abide the prescribed laws, rules, and regulations in revising the Annual Budget and Annual Investment Programs.

Recommendation
We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive requires the responsible Municipal Officials to immediately submit the pertinent documents of the above projects to facilitate the technical review and inspections, and moving forward, strictly comply with the above-mentioned regulations. Also, the management to identify the cause of non-submission and take necessary action to solve the issue.

Recommendations
We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer/Cashier/Accountant be directed to submit the transaction documents such as Disbursement Vouchers, Payrolls, Official Receipts, JEVs for adjustments, liquidations, etc. and their corresponding supporting documents, within the reglementary period to ensure timely evaluation of same and those deficiencies or other observations will also be immediately corrected or adjusted.

Recommendation
We recommended and the management agreed that the LCE direct the person in-charge to submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Office of the Auditor for easy audit verification promptly.

Recommendation
We recommended and management agreed that management observe the existing GPPB Circular and take notice of the GPPB opinion relative to their existing procurement policy involving businesses owned by the employees of the LGU to avoid conflict of interest in the ensuing procurement activities.

Recommendation
We recommended and management agreed to direct the BAC Secretariat to enroll in the GPPB Online Portal and post the necessary information thereat and in conspicuous places of the LGU in support of transparency and accountability in the conduct of its procurement for ancillary supplies.

Recommendation
We recommend that the Management prepare and submit the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances with supporting documents to the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC), copy furnished the Audit Team for all donations received, COVID-19 or non-COVID-19 related supplies and equipment from the private companies in the calendar year 2021 and every quarter thereafter if there is any.

Recommendation:
We recommend that the management submit written justification and the lacking supporting documents. Otherwise, irregular transactions will suspended/disallowed in audit.
We also recommend that concerned personnel provide sufficient details in the Purchase Request, Purchase Order, and Inspection and Acceptance Report.
Further, strictly observe and comply with established laws, rules and regulations specifically on documentary requirements in the disbursement of public funds, pursuant to Section 4 of Presidential Decree No. 1445, among other pertinent provisions.

We recommend that the Municipal Assessor prepare an updated data on real properties within the municipality, and furnish the Municipal Treasurer for the preparation/updating of the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card.

We further recommend that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the preparation of the necessary accounting entries to set-up the actual Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable.

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant revisit Sections 36 and 93 of the NGAS Volume 1 on the pro–forma accounting entries for income, collection and deposit, including pertinent entries for Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax.

Recommendation:
We recommend that management require the Inventory Committee to prioritize and complete the physical inventory within a reasonable period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006. Also, management is enjoined to ensure the full implementation of “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” as provided for in the same circular.

Recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant ensures that the correct amount of the current year’s unexpended Quick Response Fund (QRF) and LDRRMF-MOOE will be recorded as Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds in order to present the correct Surplus/Net Income of the current year. Since the transfer to the Special Trust Fund was deficient at year-end, management need to prepare the following entries in the ensuing year:
General Fund:	Dr Prior Period Adjustment       		₱17,500.00
Cr Due to Other Funds (or Cash in Bank if actual)  ₱17,500.00
Trust Fund:		Dr Due from Other Funds (or Cash in Bank if actual) ₱17,500.00
Cr Trust Liabilities – DRRMF    	     ₱17,500.00
We also recommend that the management effect the actual transfer of funds from Trust Fund to General Fund and prepare the following journal entries in the succeeding year to correct the account balances:
Trust Fund:		Dr Trust Liabilities – DRRMF    		₱994,826.54
Cr Cash in Bank					₱994,826.54
General Fund: 	Dr Cash in Bank		      		₱994,826.54

Recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entry to reflect the correct balance by debiting Miscellaneous Income and credit to Prior Period Adjustments, both amounting to ₱14,134,826.54. If books are already closed, no accounting entry will be prepared as the Miscellaneous Income and the Prior Period Adjustments are nominal accounts, closed to Government Equity.
We further recommend that Municipal Accountant revisit the currently applicable New Government Accounting System Manual and discontinue the practice of adjusting deficits by recognizing again as income those already recognized in previous years.

Recommendation:
We recommend that information on the loss and damage to properties be furnished to the Municipal Accountant in order to disclose the details in the Notes to Financial Statements.
We further recommend that management require all department/offices to submit a list of lost and damaged properties to the General Service Office. The lists have to be traced to the Property Acknowledgment Receipt/Acknowledgment Receipt of Equipment for the preparation of the requests from relief of accountability (for lost items) or Inventory and Inspection of Unserviceable Property (for damaged items due for disposal).

Recommendation:
We recommend that management submit written justifications together with the related documents on the above-noted findings. Otherwise, irregular/illegal expenditures shall be disallowed in audit pursuant to Items 3.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.
We also recommend that programs, projects and activities (PPAs) be implemented with prior authority from the Sangguniang Bayan, specifically for budget items that are described in generic terms.
Further, we recommend that management strictly observes the provision in DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 in the identification of allowable projects of the 20% Development Fund.

Recommendation:
We recommend further that management strictly observe and comply with established laws, rules and regulations to safeguard government funds, specifically on RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-003.

Recommendation:
We recommend that the General Services Office (GSO) submit written justification in the preparation of Purchase Request with apparent exorbitant prices without checking/reference to the market prices.
We also recommend that the management submit written justification/comment on the excess amounting to ₱104,331.48 of the contract price versus the COA evaluated cost. Otherwise, excessive expenditure shall be disallowed in audit.

Recommendation:
We recommend that management limit the hiring of casuals/job order employees for intermittent, emergency jobs or specific piece of work with short duration as guided by CSC-COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; and maximize the services of the permanent employees for regular agency functions.
We also recommend that management direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to charge and record the wages of the contractual and job order workers performing regular agency functions to Personal Services.

Recommendation:
We recommend that Management require the concerned municipal officials/employee to immediately submit the deficient financial reports and ensure that the prescribed periods in the submission of reports are observed. Failure to submit these reports on time shall automatically cause the suspension of payment of their salaries pursuant to Sections 122 of PD 1445.

Recommendation:
We recommend that management assign personnel who will prepare the report and submit as soon as possible its AAPSI for the AAR 2020.
Further, we recommended that management comply with the provision and submit the action plan within sixty (60) days upon receipt of the AAR in the ensuing years.

Recommendation:
We recommend that Local Chief Executive issue an executive order reconstituting the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), and enumerating the general functions as well as their roles and responsibilities under Section 4.1.A of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.
We also recommend that all members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads undertake capacity building on GAD, which shall include but not limited to gender sensitivity training, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis and GAD tools.
We further recommend that management, through the GFPS, initiate the development of GAD Database and three-year GAD Agenda in order to achieve a gender-responsive local governance.

We recommended that management:
Require the OIC- Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Treasurer to make a realistic projection on “Other Income-RPC” in the succeeding year; and
Intensify collection of all identified revenue sources to meet/surpass the targeted amount of collections that will finance additional program, projects and activities for the benefit of the constituents of the Municipality.

We recommended that management ensure that all disbursements/utilization under the 20% Development Fund is in conformity with the guidelines set by Department of the Interior and Local Government and Department of Budget and Management (DILG-DBM) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 01 dated March 27, 2020. We further recommended the refund of irregular transaction amounting to ₱19,931.78. Corresponding Notice of Disallowance will be issued when necessary.

We recommended that management:
Submit a written explanation why the Revenue Code of the Municipality has not been updated since 2011; and
Update the Revenue Code of the Municipality of Alicia, Isabela pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160 to increase the revenue collection from local taxes that can be used to finance more valuable Projects/Programs/Activities of the Municipality.

We recommended that management require (1) the Petty Cash Custodian and Accountant to reconcile the accounts pertaining to the petty cash fund transaction and make necessary adjustment in the books pursuant to prescribed Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units as provided under COA Circular 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; or (2) discontinue the fund under the Petty Cash Custodian as it is redundant with the Petty Cash Fund maintained by the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that management:
Ensure that all disbursements for financial assistance should be supported with appropriate and complete documentation as required in Section 4 (6) of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012;
All cases should be referred to the office of MSWD for evaluation and case study report that should form part of the supporting documents of the claim; and
Provide the necessary supporting documents for the financial assistance already granted by the Petty Cash Custodian to avoid the risk of suspension/ disallowances of transactions.

We recommended that management:
Require the Accountable Officers to maintain official cashbook to provide proper recording of their transactions and to facilitate immediate and easy verification of cash accountability at any given time, as well as to enhance reporting and monitoring;
Require the Accountable Officers to closely monitor and ensure the accuracy and physical existence of cash on hand by reconciling daily the cash on hand with the cash balance in the books; and
Require the Accountable Officers and Accountant to reconcile their books of accounts at the end of the month.

We recommended that management apply for additional bond from the Fidelity Fund Division, Bureau of the Treasury, to increase the fidelity bond of the Accountable Officer that commensurate to her accountabilities, in accordance with Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to provide adequate protection on Municipality’s resources.

We recommended that management submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund using the format prescribed under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in order to monitor compliance on accounting and reporting of the utilization of LDRRMF.

We recommend that (1) the Municipality submit an explanation or justification as to the reason why RED CAT INTERNATIONAL LTD. was named the beneficiary in the LC instead of AVANTRAC Heavy Machinery, Inc., the supplier in whose name the contract was awarded; and, (2) the Municipality refrain from facilitating the availability of a letter of credit to a foreign entity, who is not a winning bidder and not a party to the contract by and between the Municipality of Alicia, under section 43.1.1 of RA 9184 and NPM 051-2004, dated April 16, 2004, of the Non-Policy Opinions.

We recommend that the management discontinue accepting collection receipts in place of the sales invoice or official receipts as proof of payment. On the other hand, we recommend that the Municipal Treasurer demand from the supplier for the sales invoices or official receipts to obtain principal evidence on the payment of goods.

We recommend that the Municipality comply with the mandates prescribed under Local Budget Circular (LBC) 125 and submit the monthly report using the prescribed format (Annex C) to the auditor's office.

We recommend that the Management make sure that the documents required to support claim against government funds are proper and complete. We also recommend that cash advance for the payment of financial assistance should not be included with the cash advances for payroll. The request for financial aid must be in the name of the person in need of help.

We recommend that management to strictly compliance with relevant provisions of laws and rules governing the administration of LDRRMF for government resources to be assured of having been administered and appropriately used, regularly, and legally according to the purposes for which the fund was appropriated.

We recommend that management direct the timely submission of the Monthly Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF by the MDRRMO- designate as certified correct by the Municipal Accountant to the Office of the Auditor as required under item 25, Section 4, Rule 6 of IRR of RA 10121 and detailed under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommend that management submit written justification on the procurement of medical supplies with prices exceeding the price ceiling provided by the Department of Health. Otherwise, require the supplier to refund the excess amount computed based on the DOH Memorandum Nos. 2020-0144A and 2020-0250A.  

We further recommend that management observe the Drug Price Reference Index and other related issuances of the Department of Health in the succeeding procurement of medicines and medical supplies pursuant to Republic Act No. 7581 and DOH Administrative Order No. 2014-0001.
